bibliographic_data.title: On Statistical Analysis of MOEAs with Multiple Performance Indicators
bibliographic_data.abstract: Assessing the empirical performance of Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) is vital when testing a set of MOEAs and determining their ranking. Multiple performance indicators, such as generational distance and hypervolume, are frequently used but typically analyzed independently. This paper proposes conducting a multivariate E-test on the joint empirical distribution of performance indicators to detect potential differences among algorithms, followed by a post-hoc procedure using linear discriminative analysis.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes conducting a multivariate E-test on the joint empirical distribution of performance indicators to detect potential differences among algorithms, followed by a post-hoc procedure using linear discriminative analysis.
core_content.key_methodology: A multivariate E-test is proposed for analyzing the joint empirical distribution of multiple performance indicators and a post-hoc procedure utilizing linear discriminative analysis to determine algorithm superiority.
outcomes: Detection of potential differences among algorithms
Identification of superior algorithms based on performance indicators
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the challenge of assessing the empirical performance of Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) by proposing a multivariate E-test to analyze the joint empirical distribution of multiple performance indicators. This approach aims to overcome the limitations of analyzing each indicator independently, which can fail to detect significant differences among algorithms if their marginal distributions overlap. The proposed method involves conducting a multivariate E-test followed by a post-hoc procedure using linear discriminative analysis to determine algorithm superiority. The effectiveness of this performance analysis is demonstrated through an experiment involving four algorithms, 16 problems, and six different numbers of objectives.
